body_txt: An episode focused primarily on otherwise minor characters, possibly recurring characters or actual guest stars we've never seen before , but using their point of view to give an outsider's perspective on the main cast or a typical plot handled by the main cast. It works well in countering the assumption that The Main Characters Do Everything , while also implying Hero of Another Story . Not coincidentally, the principal actors are needed a lot less for this sort of episode than in a typical episode. Lower Deck Episodes sometimes arise when the crew is behind on their film schedules and have to shoot a Bottle Episode or piggyback two episodes at the same time note Depending on contracts actors may get less pay if their normal role is reduced for an episode, and conversely the pay rate of a day player or guest star may still let the episode come in under budget . The main cast are seldom entirely absent, since they have to get their Mandatory Line in somewhere. Named for " Lower Decks ," episode #167 of Star Trek: The Next Generation , an episode that is notable for focusing on a group of lower ranked crewmen. The ensigns were concerned about upcoming performance evaluations, the actual lead actors were relegated to a B-Plot that still focused on the evaluations, as well as a C-Plot that under normal circumstances would have been the main plot but the characters don't have the security clearance to know what is going on. Short films of this nature are sometimes included as a special feature for the DVD/Blu-Ray release of Hollywood films, particularly animated films. See A Day in the Limelight for a secondary character given the spotlight and Villain Episode for villains. See Breakout Mook Character for full spinoffs for mooks. Compare Elsewhere Fic . May overlap with The Greatest Story Never Told and Poorly Disguised Pilot . An entire series of Lower Deck Episodes (within a larger 'verse ) is an Innocent Bystander Series . See also a Gaiden Game .
